excerpts: Deterministic execution unlocks durability. If the Workflow has to recover from a crash, it “replays” your agent’s progress to date. But the Workflow does not ask the agent for a new plan for decisions it has already made... The agent instead uses Temporal’s Event History as a record of past decisions. This makes the execution **durable** and is what makes the agent able to survive a crash and resume exactly where it left off.

Your **Workflow** is the orchestration layer, the blueprint that defines the structure of your application. It needs to be deterministic so Temporal can help your agent survive through process crashes, outages, and other failures.
**While Temporal requires that your Workflow code is deterministic, your AI Agent can absolutely make decisions based on non-deterministic LLM outcomes.**

This separation is *exactly* what makes Temporal perfect for AI agents. In fact, the longer running your agents are, the more valuable Temporal’s [Durable Execution](https://temporal.io/blog/what-is-durable-execution) superpowers become.
**Deterministic in execution** : An agent will always take the same path given the same context and LLM decisions
**BUT NOT predetermined** : You don't know what the LLM will decide until runtime

excerpts: Multi-agent architecture enables several powerful patterns. Here, I’ll start from the basics, and describe how Temporal can be used to make building multi-agent systems simple, durable, and fun.
One question I get asked often is, *“Should I build an agent as a Workflow or as an Activity?”*
For me, the answer is: it depends on *lifecycle and interaction:*
**Conversational Agents:** interactive, long running, orchestration: Workflows
**LLM Calls:** call to external system: Activities
**Simple Automation Agents:** short-lived, no interaction: Activities
**Long-Running Agents:** long duration, often have interaction: Workflows

excerpts:
With this update, LangSmith now offers a complete OpenTelemetry pipeline for LLM applications:
**LangChain instrumentation** : Automatically generate detailed traces from your LangChain or LangGraph applications
**LangSmith SDK** : Convert and transport these traces through our SDK using OpenTelemetry's standardized format
**LangSmith platform** : Ingest and visualize traces in a powerful, LLM-specific observability dashboard
This end-to-end integration unlocks several key benefits:
**Unified observability** : View your entire application stack—from LangChain components to underlying infrastructure—in a single, cohesive view
**Distributed tracing** : Follow requests as they move through your microservices architecture, with context propagation ensuring that related spans are linked to the same trace
**Interoperability** : Connect LangSmith with your existing observability tools and infrastructure through the OpenTelemetry standard, including platforms like Datadog, Grafana, and Jaeger
With this integration, you can trace the complete execution path of your LLM applications, from the initial prompt to the final response, with detailed visibility into each step along the way.
OpenTelemetry (OTel) is an open-source observability framework  that standardizes how telemetry data is collected, exported, and analyzed
For LLM applications, observability presents unique challenges. Traditional application monitoring focuses on errors and compliance with expected behaviors — however, LLM observability requires understanding multi-step workflows and monitoring dynamic, stochastic outputs with complex evaluation metrics that go beyond simple error rates.
OpenTelemetry addresses these challenges by providing a unified, vendor-neutral standard for instrumentation that works across different languages, frameworks, and backends.
LangSmith now provides end-to-end OpenTelemetry (OTel) support for applications built on LangChain and/or LangGraph.
With our OpenTelemetry (OTel) integration, you can standardize tracing across your stack and send traces to LangSmith — our testing & observability platform for the agent lifecycle — or other observability platforms.

excerpts: In OpenTelemetry terms, the entire agent loop is the root span. Each LLM call and tool execution is a child span.
The parent-child relationships between spans capture the execution hierarchy. And the trace as a whole tells you the full story of how the agent processed a request.

ith tracer.start_as_current_span(f"agent.llm_step") as llm_span:
    llm_span.set_attribute("agent.iteration", iteration)
    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.system", "openai")
    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.model", "gpt-4o")
When you have multiple agents collaborating, span links let you connect related traces without implying a parent-child relationship.

| Attribute | Description |
| `agent.iteration` | Current iteration number in the agent loop |
| `agent.decision` | What the agent decided (tool_call, final_answer) |
| `agent.tool.name` | Name of the tool being executed |
| `gen_ai.usage.input_tokens` | Tokens consumed by each LLM call |
| `gen_ai.usage.output_tokens` | Tokens generated by each LLM call |
Each tool call deserves its own span. This lets you see how long each tool takes and what arguments were passed.

excerpts: **You can’t define success.** Agents need termination conditions. If “done” is fuzzy or subjective, the agent will either stop too early or run forever.

**The Short Answer
** Combine goal-completion detection, iteration limits, time limits and explicit “I’m stuck” recognition — with the orchestrator as the final authority.
*

.
**Layered termination strategy:

Layer 1: LLM self-assessment.** The agent explicitly evaluates whether the goal is achieved. This is a separate reasoning step, not embedded in the main loop: “Given the original goal and current state, is the task complete? Why or why not?”

**Layer 2: Programmatic verification.** Where possible, verify completion programmatically. If the goal was “create a file containing X,” check that the file exists and contains X. Don’t trust the agent’s claim.
**Layer 3: Progress detection.** Track whether the agent is making meaningful progress. If the last N steps haven’t changed the environment state meaningfully, prompt for clarification or terminate.
**Layer 4: Hard limits.** Absolute caps on iterations, time and cost. These fire regardless of what the agent thinks.
**Layer 5: Stuck detection.** Recognize patterns that indicate the agent can’t proceed: repeated errors, circular reasoning, repeated tool calls with same arguments. Route these to human review.

**The Complete Answer
** Over-reasoning is one of the most common failure modes I see. The agent thinks and thinks and thinks but never acts, or it creates elaborate plans for simple tasks

durable execution enables a process to resume without reprocessing previous steps — even after a significant delay (e.g., a week later).
`"exit"` : LangGraph persists changes only when graph execution exits either successfully, with an error, or due to a human in the loop interrupt.
`"async"` : LangGraph persists changes asynchronously while the next step executes.
`"sync"` : LangGraph persists changes synchronously before the next step starts.

excerpts: **Durable execution** is a technique in which a process or workflow saves its progress at key points, allowing it to pause and later resume exactly where it left off.
LangGraph’s built-in persistence layer provides durable execution for workflows, ensuring that the state of each execution step is saved to a durable store.
durable execution enables a process to resume without reprocessing previous steps — even after a significant delay (e.g., a week later).
If you are using LangGraph with a checkpointer, you already have durable execution enabled.
To make the most of durable execution, ensure that your workflow is designed to be deterministic and idempotent and wrap any side effects or non-deterministic operations inside tasks
Enable persistence in your workflow by specifying a checkpointer that will save workflow progress.
Specify a thread identifier when executing a workflow. This will track the execution history for a particular instance of the workflow.

When you resume a workflow run, the code does **NOT** resume from the **same line of code** where execution stopped; instead, it will identify an appropriate starting point from which to pick up where it left off.
`"exit"` : LangGraph persists changes only when graph execution exits either successfully, with an error, or due to a human in the loop interrupt.
`"async"` : LangGraph persists changes asynchronously while the next step executes.
`"sync"` : LangGraph persists changes synchronously before the next step starts.

excerpts:
With this update, LangSmith now offers a complete OpenTelemetry pipeline for LLM applications:
**LangChain instrumentation** : Automatically generate detailed traces from your LangChain or LangGraph applications
**LangSmith SDK** : Convert and transport these traces through our SDK using OpenTelemetry's standardized format
**LangSmith platform** : Ingest and visualize traces in a powerful, LLM-specific observability dashboard
This end-to-end integration unlocks several key benefits:
**Unified observability** : View your entire application stack—from LangChain components to underlying infrastructure—in a single, cohesive view
**Distributed tracing** : Follow requests as they move through your microservices architecture, with context propagation ensuring that related spans are linked to the same trace
**Interoperability** : Connect LangSmith with your existing observability tools and infrastructure through the OpenTelemetry standard, including platforms like Datadog, Grafana, and Jaeger
With this integration, you can trace the complete execution path of your LLM applications, from the initial prompt to the final response, with detailed visibility into each step along the way.
OpenTelemetry (OTel) is an open-source observability framework  that standardizes how telemetry data is collected, exported, and analyzed
For LLM applications, observability presents unique challenges. Traditional application monitoring focuses on errors and compliance with expected behaviors — however, LLM observability requires understanding multi-step workflows and monitoring dynamic, stochastic outputs with complex evaluation metrics that go beyond simple error rates.
OpenTelemetry addresses these challenges by providing a unified, vendor-neutral standard for instrumentation that works across different languages, frameworks, and backends.
LangSmith now provides end-to-end OpenTelemetry (OTel) support for applications built on LangChain and/or LangGraph.
With our OpenTelemetry (OTel) integration, you can standardize tracing across your stack and send traces to LangSmith — our testing & observability platform for the agent lifecycle — or other observability platforms.
ngSmith platform** : Ingest and visualize traces in a powerful, LLM-specific observability dashboard

Related content about durable execution, observability, and budget governance provides context for how termination controls interact with system resilience and cost management, but the strongest evidence for the specific field value lies in the explicit termination patterns and detection mechanisms described in the layered termination constructs and the explicit enumeration of failure modes. excerpts: **You can’t define success.** Agents need termination conditions. If “done” is fuzzy or subjective, the agent will either stop too early or run forever.

.
**Layered termination strategy:

Layer 1: LLM self-assessment.** The agent explicitly evaluates whether the goal is achieved. This is a separate reasoning step, not embedded in the main loop: “Given the original goal and current state, is the task complete? Why or why not?”

**Layer 2: Programmatic verification.** Where possible, verify completion programmatically. If the goal was “create a file containing X,” check that the file exists and contains X. Don’t trust the agent’s claim.
**Layer 3: Progress detection.** Track whether the agent is making meaningful progress. If the last N steps haven’t changed the environment state meaningfully, prompt for clarification or terminate.
**Layer 4: Hard limits.** Absolute caps on iterations, time and cost. These fire regardless of what the agent thinks.
**Layer 5: Stuck detection.** Recognize patterns that indicate the agent can’t proceed: repeated errors, circular reasoning, repeated tool calls with same arguments. Route these to human review.

**The Complete Answer
** Over-reasoning is one of the most common failure modes I see. The agent thinks and thinks and thinks but never acts, or it creates elaborate plans for simple tasks

excerpts: In OpenTelemetry terms, the entire agent loop is the root span. Each LLM call and tool execution is a child span.
The parent-child relationships between spans capture the execution hierarchy. And the trace as a whole tells you the full story of how the agent processed a request.

ith tracer.start_as_current_span(f"agent.llm_step") as llm_span:
    llm_span.set_attribute("agent.iteration", iteration)
    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.system", "openai")
    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.model", "gpt-4o")
When you have multiple agents collaborating, span links let you connect related traces without implying a parent-child relationship.

| Attribute | Description |
| `agent.iteration` | Current iteration number in the agent loop |
| `agent.decision` | What the agent decided (tool_call, final_answer) |
| `agent.tool.name` | Name of the tool being executed |
| `gen_ai.usage.input_tokens` | Tokens consumed by each LLM call |
| `gen_ai.usage.output_tokens` | Tokens generated by each LLM call |
Each tool call deserves its own span. This lets you see how long each tool takes and what arguments were passed.

excerpts:
.
**Layered termination strategy:

**Layer 2: Programmatic verification.** Where possible, verify completion programmatically. If the goal was “create a file containing X,” check that the file exists and contains X. Don’t trust the agent’s claim.

Layer 1: LLM self-assessment.** The agent explicitly evaluates whether the goal is achieved. This is a separate reasoning step, not embedded in the main loop: “Given the original goal and current state, is the task complete? Why or why not?”
**Layer 4: Hard limits.** Absolute caps on iterations, time and cost. These fire regardless of what the agent thinks.
**Layer 5: Stuck detection.** Recognize patterns that indicate the agent can’t proceed: repeated errors, circular reasoning, repeated tool calls with same arguments. Route these to human review.

**The Complete Answer
** Over-reasoning is one of the most common failure modes I see. The agent thinks and thinks and thinks but never acts, or it creates elaborate plans for simple tasks

excerpts: In OpenTelemetry terms, the entire agent loop is the root span. Each LLM call and tool execution is a child span.
The parent-child relationships between spans capture the execution hierarchy. And the trace as a whole tells you the full story of how the agent processed a request.

ith tracer.start_as_current_span(f"agent.llm_step") as llm_span:
    llm_span.set_attribute("agent.iteration", iteration)
    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.system", "openai")
    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.model", "gpt-4o")

| Attribute | Description |
| `agent.iteration` | Current iteration number in the agent loop |
| `agent.decision` | What the agent decided (tool_call, final_answer) |
| `agent.tool.name` | Name of the tool being executed |
| `gen_ai.usage.input_tokens` | Tokens consumed by each LLM call |
| `gen_ai.usage.output_tokens` | Tokens generated by each LLM call |
Each tool call deserves its own span. This lets you see how long each tool takes and what arguments were passed.

excerpts:
With this update, LangSmith now offers a complete OpenTelemetry pipeline for LLM applications:
**LangChain instrumentation** : Automatically generate detailed traces from your LangChain or LangGraph applications
**LangSmith SDK** : Convert and transport these traces through our SDK using OpenTelemetry's standardized format
**LangSmith platform** : Ingest and visualize traces in a powerful, LLM-specific observability dashboard
This end-to-end integration unlocks several key benefits:
**Unified observability** : View your entire application stack—from LangChain components to underlying infrastructure—in a single, cohesive view
**Distributed tracing** : Follow requests as they move through your microservices architecture, with context propagation ensuring that related spans are linked to the same trace
**Interoperability** : Connect LangSmith with your existing observability tools and infrastructure through the OpenTelemetry standard, including platforms like Datadog, Grafana, and Jaeger
With this integration, you can trace the complete execution path of your LLM applications, from the initial prompt to the final response, with detailed visibility into each step along the way.
OpenTelemetry (OTel) is an open-source observability framework  that standardizes how telemetry data is collected, exported, and analyzed
LangSmith now provides end-to-end OpenTelemetry (OTel) support for applications built on LangChain and/or LangGraph.
With our OpenTelemetry (OTel) integration, you can standardize tracing across your stack and send traces to LangSmith — our testing & observability platform for the agent lifecycle — or other observability platforms.

excerpts: **Durable execution** is a technique in which a process or workflow saves its progress at key points, allowing it to pause and later resume exactly where it left off.
LangGraph’s built-in persistence layer provides durable execution for workflows, ensuring that the state of each execution step is saved to a durable store.
durable execution enables a process to resume without reprocessing previous steps — even after a significant delay (e.g., a week later).
If you are using LangGraph with a checkpointer, you already have durable execution enabled.
To make the most of durable execution, ensure that your workflow is designed to be deterministic and idempotent and wrap any side effects or non-deterministic operations inside tasks
`"exit"` : LangGraph persists changes only when graph execution exits either successfully, with an error, or due to a human in the loop interrupt.

excerpts: **Checkpointing** : AI applications beyond the simplest of demos make many calls to LLMs and tools, and they are increasingly leveraging other agents. In the event that the application stops before completion (due to both foreseen and unforeseen causes), checkpointing keeps the application from having to start from the beginning and rerun previous steps in the workflow.

ree.
If the application instance were to shut down (crash, be proactively cycled, or because you wanted to fix a bug even while the app was running), as soon as the application starts again, the state will be recreated and processing will pick up where it left off. When building Temporal applications,

For example, when a network glitch renders the LLM unavailable or when the LLM is rate-limiting requests, the Activity automatically retries those requests until conditions allow for completion. As the developer, you don’t write the retry logic; that behavior is handled by Temporal.
citations.title: Durable Execution meets AI: Why Temporal is ideal for AI agents & Generative AI Apps | Temporal

excerpts: Deterministic execution unlocks durability. If the Workflow has to recover from a crash, it “replays” your agent’s progress to date. But the Workflow does not ask the agent for a new plan for decisions it has already made... The agent instead uses Temporal’s Event History as a record of past decisions. This makes the execution **durable** and is what makes the agent able to survive a crash and resume exactly where it left off.
This gives us non-deterministic Agents with durable execution. And this is *exactly* the type of problem Temporal is primed to solve.
**While Temporal requires that your Workflow code is deterministic, your AI Agent can absolutely make decisions based on non-deterministic LLM outcomes.**

This separation is *exactly* what makes Temporal perfect for AI agents. In fact, the longer running your agents are, the more valuable Temporal’s [Durable Execution](https://temporal.io/blog/what-is-durable-execution) superpowers become.
citations.title: Of course you can build dynamic AI agents with Temporal | Temporal

excerpts: Multi-agent architecture enables several powerful patterns. Here, I’ll start from the basics, and describe how Temporal can be used to make building multi-agent systems simple, durable, and fun.
One question I get asked often is, *“Should I build an agent as a Workflow or as an Activity?”*
For me, the answer is: it depends on *lifecycle and interaction:*
**Conversational Agents:** interactive, long running, orchestration: Workflows
**LLM Calls:** call to external system: Activities
**Simple Automation Agents:** short-lived, no interaction: Activities
**Long-Running Agents:** long duration, often have interaction: Workflows
**Proactive Agents:** interactive, long-running: Workflows

excerpts:
**The Speed Limit (Usage Throttling):** A **Limit of executions per minute** prevents "runaway" usage or an application bug from generating excessive, rapid calls.
**The Time Limit (Efficiency Control):** A **Time limit (hs)** (e.g., 24 hours) for a single conversation ensures that an agent isn't held in an open, expensive state indefinitely, which can consume unnecessary tokens.
**Monthly quota limit ($)** |Implements a **Hard Cap** on total monthly spend (The "Run" cost)
**Dynamic Model Selection:** Route simple, high-volume tasks (like FAQs) to cheaper models (e.g., GPT-4 mini) and reserve powerful models (like **Claude 4 Sonnet** ) only for complex reasoning tasks.
*Execution limits per minute** |Implements **API Rate Limiting** . Prevents excessive, rapid calls that could lead to budget spikes and API provider penalties.

excerpts: LangSmith automatically records LLM token usage and costs for major providers, and also allows you to submit custom cost data for any additional components. This gives you a single, unified view of costs across your entire application, which makes it easy to monitor, understand, and debug your spend.

In the [LangSmith UI](https://smith.langchain.com) , you can explore usage and spend in three main ways: first by understanding how tokens and costs are broken down, then by viewing those details within individual traces, and finally by inspecting aggregated metrics in project stats and dashboards.
S

Token usage and costs are broken down into three categories:
**Input** : Tokens in the prompt sent to the model. Subtypes include: cache reads, text tokens, image tokens, etc
**Output** : Tokens generated in the response from the model. Subtypes include: reasoning tokens, text tokens, image tokens, etc
**Other** : Costs from tool calls, retrieval steps or any custom runs.
You can view detailed breakdowns by hovering over cost sections in the UI. When available, each section is further categorized by subtype.
You can add prices for other models , or overwrite pricing for default models if you have custom pricing
compute cost automatically from token usage, you need to provide **token counts** , the **model and provider** and the **mode
A model pricing map is used to map model names to their per-token prices to compute costs from token counts.

is being discussed in [this OpenTelemetry issue](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions/issues/1530) and aims to establish a standardized approach for frameworks such as IBM Bee
Stack, IBM wxFlow, CrewAI, AutoGen, LangGraph, and others.

Given that observability and evaluation tools for GenAI come from various
vendors, it is important to establish standards around the shape of the
telemetry generated by agent apps to avoid lock-in caused by vendor or framework
specific formats.

As AI agent ecosystems continue to mature, the need for standardized and robust
observability has become more apparent. While some frameworks offer built-in
instrumentation, others rely on integration with observability tools. This
fragmented landscape underscores the importance of the [GenAI observability project](https://github.com/open-telemetry/community/blob/5125996b5d159ff9aaa906f9a25226a821dc7bed/projects/gen-ai.md?from_branch=main) and OpenTelemetry’s emerging semantic conventions, which aim to unify how
telemetry data is collected and reported.
citations.title: AI Agent Observability - Evolving Standards and Best Practices | OpenTelemetry

excerpts:
With this update, LangSmith now offers a complete OpenTelemetry pipeline for LLM applications:
**LangChain instrumentation** : Automatically generate detailed traces from your LangChain or LangGraph applications
**LangSmith SDK** : Convert and transport these traces through our SDK using OpenTelemetry's standardized format
**LangSmith platform** : Ingest and visualize traces in a powerful, LLM-specific observability dashboard
This end-to-end integration unlocks several key benefits:
**Unified observability** : View your entire application stack—from LangChain components to underlying infrastructure—in a single, cohesive view
**Distributed tracing** : Follow requests as they move through your microservices architecture, with context propagation ensuring that related spans are linked to the same trace
**Interoperability** : Connect LangSmith with your existing observability tools and infrastructure through the OpenTelemetry standard, including platforms like Datadog, Grafana, and Jaeger
With this integration, you can trace the complete execution path of your LLM applications, from the initial prompt to the final response, with detailed visibility into each step along the way.
OpenTelemetry (OTel) is an open-source observability framework  that standardizes how telemetry data is collected, exported, and analyzed
For LLM applications, observability presents unique challenges. Traditional application monitoring focuses on errors and compliance with expected behaviors — however, LLM observability requires understanding multi-step workflows and monitoring dynamic, stochastic outputs with complex evaluation metrics that go beyond simple error rates.
OpenTelemetry addresses these challenges by providing a unified, vendor-neutral standard for instrumentation that works across different languages, frameworks, and backends.
LangSmith now provides end-to-end OpenTelemetry (OTel) support for applications built on LangChain and/or LangGraph.
With our OpenTelemetry (OTel) integration, you can standardize tracing across your stack and send traces to LangSmith — our testing & observability platform for the agent lifecycle — or other observability platforms.
citations.title: Introducing End-to-End OpenTelemetry Support in LangSmith

excerpts:
**🎉 New** :
Our semantic conventions are now part of OpenTelemetry! Join the [discussion](https://github.com/open-telemetry/community/blob/1c71595874e5d125ca92ec3b0e948c4325161c8a/projects/llm-semconv.md) and help us shape the future of LLM observability.
OpenLLMetry is a set of extensions built on top of [OpenTelemetry](https://opentelemetry.io/) that gives you complete observability over your LLM application.

Because it uses OpenTelemetry under the hood, [it can be connected to your existing observability solutions](https://www.traceloop.com/docs/openllmetry/integrations/introduction) - Datadog, Honeycomb, and others.

The repo contains standard OpenTelemetry instrumentations for LLM providers and Vector DBs, as well as a Traceloop SDK that makes it easy to get started with OpenLLMetry, while still outputting standard OpenTelemetry data that can be connected to your observability stack.

excerpts: For multi-agent or tool-based frameworks, attributes such as `gen_ai.operation.name` (e.g., `tool_call` or `agent_run` ) let you trace end-to-end agent and tool flows within orchestrated AI systems.

Datadog automatically maps GenAI attributes (e.g., `gen_ai.request.model` , `gen_ai.usage.input_tokens` , `gen_ai.provider.name` , and `gen_ai.operation.name` ) to the native LLM Observability schema for latency, token usage, cost, model/provider, and finish reason.

They also capture latency and cost metrics derived from span duration and provider metadata, as well as finish reasons and details about tool or agent operations to provide comprehensive workflow visibility.
citations.title: Datadog LLM Observability natively supports OpenTelemetry GenAI Semantic Conventions | Datadog

excerpts: In OpenTelemetry terms, the entire agent loop is the root span. Each LLM call and tool execution is a child span.
The parent-child relationships between spans capture the execution hierarchy. And the trace as a whole tells you the full story of how the agent processed a request.

ith tracer.start_as_current_span(f"agent.llm_step") as llm_span:
    llm_span.set_attribute("agent.iteration", iteration)
    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.system", "openai")
    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.model", "gpt-4o")
When you have multiple agents collaborating, span links let you connect related traces without implying a parent-child relationship.

| Attribute | Description |
| `agent.iteration` | Current iteration number in the agent loop |
| `agent.decision` | What the agent decided (tool_call, final_answer) |
| `agent.tool.name` | Name of the tool being executed |
| `gen_ai.usage.input_tokens` | Tokens consumed by each LLM call |
| `gen_ai.usage.output_tokens` | Tokens generated by each LLM call |
Each tool call deserves its own span. This lets you see how long each tool takes and what arguments were passed.

basis.reasoning: The most relevant excerpts address explicit termination conditions and how termination is decided and enforced within an agent system. One excerpt notes that you cannot define success without clear termination conditions and that agents require termination criteria, which directly supports the concept of hard, non-negotiable caps on execution. Another excerpt outlines combining multiple explicit controls—iteration limits, time limits, and a recognition signal for being stuck—anticipating orchestrator-level enforcement to prevent runaway behavior. Additional excerpts discuss layered termination strategies and the role of the orchestrator as the final authority, reinforcing a governance pattern where termination is controlled and definitive. Related pieces describe crash recovery, deterministic replay, and durability patterns (checkpointing, state recreation after restart) that enable graceful stop and reliable resumption, which align with the broader objective of safe, observable termination and recoverable state when a breaker trips. While the excerpts do not use the exact phrase circuit breakers, the described mechanisms—caps on iterations/time/cost, failure handling, and orchestrator-enforced stopping with state preservation—map closely to the idea of circuit-breaker-style enforcement and controlled termination as a non-negotiable safeguard. Collectively, these excerpts support the core value: hard, enforceable termination limits implemented at the orchestrator level, with clear observability and recoverability pathways. The durability-focused pieces also reinforce how to gracefully stop and preserve state when a termination condition is triggered, which is essential to a robust exit strategy.

excerpts: **Checkpointing** : AI applications beyond the simplest of demos make many calls to LLMs and tools, and they are increasingly leveraging other agents. In the event that the application stops before completion (due to both foreseen and unforeseen causes), checkpointing keeps the application from having to start from the beginning and rerun previous steps in the workflow.

For example, when a network glitch renders the LLM unavailable or when the LLM is rate-limiting requests, the Activity automatically retries those requests until conditions allow for completion. As the developer, you don’t write the retry logic; that behavior is handled by Temporal.

ree.
If the application instance were to shut down (crash, be proactively cycled, or because you wanted to fix a bug even while the app was running), as soon as the application starts again, the state will be recreated and processing will pick up where it left off. When building Temporal applications,
citations.title: Durable Execution meets AI: Why Temporal is ideal for AI agents & Generative AI Apps | Temporal

excerpts: Deterministic execution unlocks durability. If the Workflow has to recover from a crash, it “replays” your agent’s progress to date. But the Workflow does not ask the agent for a new plan for decisions it has already made... The agent instead uses Temporal’s Event History as a record of past decisions. This makes the execution **durable** and is what makes the agent able to survive a crash and resume exactly where it left off.

Your **Workflow** is the orchestration layer, the blueprint that defines the structure of your application. It needs to be deterministic so Temporal can help your agent survive through process crashes, outages, and other failures.
**BUT NOT predetermined** : You don't know what the LLM will decide until runtime
**Deterministic in execution** : An agent will always take the same path given the same context and LLM decisions

This separation is *exactly* what makes Temporal perfect for AI agents. In fact, the longer running your agents are, the more valuable Temporal’s [Durable Execution](https://temporal.io/blog/what-is-durable-execution) superpowers become.
**While Temporal requires that your Workflow code is deterministic, your AI Agent can absolutely make decisions based on non-deterministic LLM outcomes.**
citations.title: Of course you can build dynamic AI agents with Temporal | Temporal

excerpts: Multi-agent architecture enables several powerful patterns. Here, I’ll start from the basics, and describe how Temporal can be used to make building multi-agent systems simple, durable, and fun.
One question I get asked often is, *“Should I build an agent as a Workflow or as an Activity?”*
For me, the answer is: it depends on *lifecycle and interaction:*
**Conversational Agents:** interactive, long running, orchestration: Workflows
**LLM Calls:** call to external system: Activities
**Simple Automation Agents:** short-lived, no interaction: Activities
**Long-Running Agents:** long duration, often have interaction: Workflows

basis.reasoning: The fine-grained field value describes Temporal’s durable execution model, anchored in event sourcing where every action and state change is captured as an immutable event in a comprehensive Event History. This Event History acts as the durable memory, enabling full reconstruction of state after failures. Checkpointing is presented as a mechanism that saves workflow progress so that, in the event of a crash or interruption, processing can resume from the last saved point rather than redoing work from the start. The architecture enforces a strict separation: workflows (deterministic orchestration) manage non-deterministic activities (LLM calls, tool usage), ensuring the orchestrator can reliably replay and reconstruct state. Recovery is described as automatic: a crashed worker can pick up execution by replaying the Event History, with automatic retries and timeouts for activities. This deterministic/non-deterministic separation, coupled with durable execution and replay semantics, underpins robust, long-running, or proactive multi-agent systems. Temporal’s guidance also notes that while workflows must be deterministic, agents can still rely on non-deterministic LLM outcomes, with the platform providing the durability layer that makes such orchestration feasible. In multi-agent contexts, these durability properties extend to the orchestration of multiple agents and activities, enabling long-running cooperation with fault tolerance and clear recovery behavior. Direct benefits highlighted include the ability to write fault-oblivious code, automated checkpointing, and built-in retry logic, which are essential for long-running, interactive or proactive multi-agent setups. The combination of these design principles directly supports the field value’s assertion about Durable Execution, Event History, deterministic Workflows, automatic Replay-based recovery, and the overall resilience benefits for AI agent orchestration and crash recovery.

excerpts: **Durable execution** is a technique in which a process or workflow saves its progress at key points, allowing it to pause and later resume exactly where it left off.
LangGraph’s built-in persistence layer provides durable execution for workflows, ensuring that the state of each execution step is saved to a durable store.
If you are using LangGraph with a checkpointer, you already have durable execution enabled.
Enable persistence in your workflow by specifying a checkpointer that will save workflow progress.

When you resume a workflow run, the code does **NOT** resume from the **same line of code** where execution stopped; instead, it will identify an appropriate starting point from which to pick up where it left off.
`"exit"` : LangGraph persists changes only when graph execution exits either successfully, with an error, or due to a human in the loop interrupt.
`"sync"` : LangGraph persists changes synchronously before the next step starts.
Specify a thread identifier when executing a workflow. This will track the execution history for a particular instance of the workflow.
To make the most of durable execution, ensure that your workflow is designed to be deterministic and idempotent and wrap any side effects or non-deterministic operations inside tasks
`"async"` : LangGraph persists changes asynchronously while the next step executes.

excerpts: Deterministic execution unlocks durability. If the Workflow has to recover from a crash, it “replays” your agent’s progress to date. But the Workflow does not ask the agent for a new plan for decisions it has already made... The agent instead uses Temporal’s Event History as a record of past decisions. This makes the execution **durable** and is what makes the agent able to survive a crash and resume exactly where it left off.

Your **Workflow** is the orchestration layer, the blueprint that defines the structure of your application. It needs to be deterministic so Temporal can help your agent survive through process crashes, outages, and other failures.

excerpts:
**The Complete Answer
** Over-reasoning is one of the most common failure modes I see. The agent thinks and thinks and thinks but never acts, or it creates elaborate plans for simple tasks
**You can’t define success.** Agents need termination conditions. If “done” is fuzzy or subjective, the agent will either stop too early or run forever.
**Layer 4: Hard limits.** Absolute caps on iterations, time and cost. These fire regardless of what the agent thinks.
**Layer 5: Stuck detection.** Recognize patterns that indicate the agent can’t proceed: repeated errors, circular reasoning, repeated tool calls with same arguments. Route these to human review.
**Layer 3: Progress detection.** Track whether the agent is making meaningful progress. If the last N steps haven’t changed the environment state meaningfully, prompt for clarification or terminate.

**Layer 2: Programmatic verification.** Where possible, verify completion programmatically. If the goal was “create a file containing X,” check that the file exists and contains X. Don’t trust the agent’s claim.

Layer 1: LLM self-assessment.** The agent explicitly evaluates whether the goal is achieved. This is a separate reasoning step, not embedded in the main loop: “Given the original goal and current state, is the task complete? Why or why not?”

excerpts: **You can’t define success.** Agents need termination conditions. If “done” is fuzzy or subjective, the agent will either stop too early or run forever.

**The Short Answer
** Combine goal-completion detection, iteration limits, time limits and explicit “I’m stuck” recognition — with the orchestrator as the final authority.
*

Layer 1: LLM self-assessment.** The agent explicitly evaluates whether the goal is achieved. This is a separate reasoning step, not embedded in the main loop: “Given the original goal and current state, is the task complete? Why or why not?”

**Layer 2: Programmatic verification.** Where possible, verify completion programmatically. If the goal was “create a file containing X,” check that the file exists and contains X. Don’t trust the agent’s claim.

.
**Layered termination strategy:
**Layer 3: Progress detection.** Track whether the agent is making meaningful progress. If the last N steps haven’t changed the environment state meaningfully, prompt for clarification or terminate.
**Layer 4: Hard limits.** Absolute caps on iterations, time and cost. These fire regardless of what the agent thinks.
**Layer 5: Stuck detection.** Recognize patterns that indicate the agent can’t proceed: repeated errors, circular reasoning, repeated tool calls with same arguments. Route these to human review.

excerpts: Your **Workflow** is the orchestration layer, the blueprint that defines the structure of your application. It needs to be deterministic so Temporal can help your agent survive through process crashes, outages, and other failures.

Deterministic execution unlocks durability. If the Workflow has to recover from a crash, it “replays” your agent’s progress to date. But the Workflow does not ask the agent for a new plan for decisions it has already made... The agent instead uses Temporal’s Event History as a record of past decisions. This makes the execution **durable** and is what makes the agent able to survive a crash and resume exactly where it left off.
**While Temporal requires that your Workflow code is deterministic, your AI Agent can absolutely make decisions based on non-deterministic LLM outcomes.**

excerpts: Multi-agent architecture enables several powerful patterns. Here, I’ll start from the basics, and describe how Temporal can be used to make building multi-agent systems simple, durable, and fun.
One question I get asked often is, *“Should I build an agent as a Workflow or as an Activity?”*
For me, the answer is: it depends on *lifecycle and interaction:*
**Conversational Agents:** interactive, long running, orchestration: Workflows
**LLM Calls:** call to external system: Activities
**Simple Automation Agents:** short-lived, no interaction: Activities
**Long-Running Agents:** long duration, often have interaction: Workflows
**Proactive Agents:** interactive, long-running: Workflows

Token usage and costs are broken down into three categories:

**Output** : Tokens generated in the response from the model. Subtypes include: reasoning tokens, text tokens, image tokens, etc
**Other** : Costs from tool calls, retrieval steps or any custom runs.

excerpts:
**The Speed Limit (Usage Throttling):** A **Limit of executions per minute** prevents "runaway" usage or an application bug from generating excessive, rapid calls.
**The Time Limit (Efficiency Control):** A **Time limit (hs)** (e.g., 24 hours) for a single conversation ensures that an agent isn't held in an open, expensive state indefinitely, which can consume unnecessary tokens.
**Monthly quota limit ($)** |Implements a **Hard Cap** on total monthly spend (The "Run" cost)
**Dynamic Model Selection:** Route simple, high-volume tasks (like FAQs) to cheaper models (e.g., GPT-4 mini) and reserve powerful models (like **Claude 4 Sonnet** ) only for complex reasoning tasks.
*Execution limits per minute** |Implements **API Rate Limiting** . Prevents excessive, rapid calls that could lead to budget spikes and API provider penalties.

excerpts:
With this update, LangSmith now offers a complete OpenTelemetry pipeline for LLM applications:
**LangChain instrumentation** : Automatically generate detailed traces from your LangChain or LangGraph applications
**LangSmith SDK** : Convert and transport these traces through our SDK using OpenTelemetry's standardized format
**LangSmith platform** : Ingest and visualize traces in a powerful, LLM-specific observability dashboard
With this integration, you can trace the complete execution path of your LLM applications, from the initial prompt to the final response, with detailed visibility into each step along the way.

excerpts: Deterministic execution unlocks durability. If the Workflow has to recover from a crash, it “replays” your agent’s progress to date. But the Workflow does not ask the agent for a new plan for decisions it has already made... The agent instead uses Temporal’s Event History as a record of past decisions. This makes the execution **durable** and is what makes the agent able to survive a crash and resume exactly where it left off.

Your **Workflow** is the orchestration layer, the blueprint that defines the structure of your application. It needs to be deterministic so Temporal can help your agent survive through process crashes, outages, and other failures.
**While Temporal requires that your Workflow code is deterministic, your AI Agent can absolutely make decisions based on non-deterministic LLM outcomes.**
citations.title: Of course you can build dynamic AI agents with Temporal | Temporal

excerpts: **Checkpointing** : AI applications beyond the simplest of demos make many calls to LLMs and tools, and they are increasingly leveraging other agents. In the event that the application stops before completion (due to both foreseen and unforeseen causes), checkpointing keeps the application from having to start from the beginning and rerun previous steps in the workflow.

ree.
If the application instance were to shut down (crash, be proactively cycled, or because you wanted to fix a bug even while the app was running), as soon as the application starts again, the state will be recreated and processing will pick up where it left off. When building Temporal applications,

For example, when a network glitch renders the LLM unavailable or when the LLM is rate-limiting requests, the Activity automatically retries those requests until conditions allow for completion. As the developer, you don’t write the retry logic; that behavior is handled by Temporal.

excerpts: LangSmith automatically records LLM token usage and costs for major providers, and also allows you to submit custom cost data for any additional components. This gives you a single, unified view of costs across your entire application, which makes it easy to monitor, understand, and debug your spend.

In the [LangSmith UI](https://smith.langchain.com) , you can explore usage and spend in three main ways: first by understanding how tokens and costs are broken down, then by viewing those details within individual traces, and finally by inspecting aggregated metrics in project stats and dashboards.
S

Token usage and costs are broken down into three categories:
**Input** : Tokens in the prompt sent to the model. Subtypes include: cache reads, text tokens, image tokens, etc
**Output** : Tokens generated in the response from the model. Subtypes include: reasoning tokens, text tokens, image tokens, etc
**Other** : Costs from tool calls, retrieval steps or any custom runs.
You can view detailed breakdowns by hovering over cost sections in the UI. When available, each section is further categorized by subtype.

compute cost automatically from token usage, you need to provide **token counts** , the **model and provider** and the **mode

excerpts: LangSmith automatically records LLM token usage and costs for major providers, and also allows you to submit custom cost data for any additional components. excerpts: Your **Workflow** is the orchestration layer, the blueprint that defines the structure of your application. It needs to be deterministic so Temporal can help your agent survive through process crashes, outages, and other failures.

Deterministic execution unlocks durability. If the Workflow has to recover from a crash, it “replays” your agent’s progress to date. But the Workflow does not ask the agent for a new plan for decisions it has already made... The agent instead uses Temporal’s Event History as a record of past decisions. This makes the execution **durable** and is what makes the agent able to survive a crash and resume exactly where it left off.

excerpts: In OpenTelemetry terms, the entire agent loop is the root span. Each LLM call and tool execution is a child span.
The parent-child relationships between spans capture the execution hierarchy. And the trace as a whole tells you the full story of how the agent processed a request.

ith tracer.start_as_current_span(f"agent.llm_step") as llm_span:
    llm_span.set_attribute("agent.iteration", iteration)
    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.system", "openai")
    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.model", "gpt-4o")
When you have multiple agents collaborating, span links let you connect related traces without implying a parent-child relationship.

| Attribute | Description |
| `agent.iteration` | Current iteration number in the agent loop |
| `agent.decision` | What the agent decided (tool_call, final_answer) |
| `agent.tool.name` | Name of the tool being executed |
| `gen_ai.usage.input_tokens` | Tokens consumed by each LLM call |
| `gen_ai.usage.output_tokens` | Tokens generated by each LLM call |
Each tool call deserves its own span. This lets you see how long each tool takes and what arguments were passed.

**Agent application semantic convention** – A draft AI agent application
semantic convention has already been established and finalized as part of the
discussions in the [OpenTelemetry semantic conventions repository

By establishing these conventions, we ensure that AI agent frameworks can report
standardized metrics, traces, and logs, making it easier to integrate

he [GenAI observability project](https://github.com/open-telemetry/community/blob/5125996b5d159ff9aaa906f9a25226a821dc7bed/projects/gen-ai.md?from_branch=main) within OpenTelemetry is actively working on defining semantic conventions to
standardize AI agent observability.

**Agent application semantic convention** – A draft AI agent application
semantic convention has already been established and finalized as part of the
discussions in the [OpenTelemetry semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions/issues/1732)

**Agent framework semantic convention** – Now, the focus has shifted towards
defining a common semantic convention for all AI agent frameworks. This effort
is being discussed in [this OpenTelemetry issue](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions/issues/1530) and aims to establish a standardized approach for frameworks such as IBM Bee
Stack, IBM wxFlow, CrewAI, AutoGen, LangGraph, and others.

Given that observability and evaluation tools for GenAI come from various
vendors, it is important to establish standards around the shape of the
telemetry generated by agent apps to avoid lock-in caused by vendor or framework
specific formats.

As AI agent ecosystems continue to mature, the need for standardized and robust
observability has become more apparent. While some frameworks offer built-in
instrumentation, others rely on integration with observability tools. This
fragmented landscape underscores the importance of the [GenAI observability project](https://github.com/open-telemetry/community/blob/5125996b5d159ff9aaa906f9a25226a821dc7bed/projects/gen-ai.md?from_branch=main) and OpenTelemetry’s emerging semantic conventions, which aim to unify how
telemetry data is collected and reported.

excerpts: Multi-agent architecture enables several powerful patterns. Here, I’ll start from the basics, and describe how Temporal can be used to make building multi-agent systems simple, durable, and fun.
One question I get asked often is, *“Should I build an agent as a Workflow or as an Activity?”*
For me, the answer is: it depends on *lifecycle and interaction:*
**Conversational Agents:** interactive, long running, orchestration: Workflows
**LLM Calls:** call to external system: Activities
**Simple Automation Agents:** short-lived, no interaction: Activities
**Long-Running Agents:** long duration, often have interaction: Workflows
**Proactive Agents:** interactive, long-running: Workflows

excerpts: LangSmith automatically records LLM token usage and costs for major providers, and also allows you to submit custom cost data for any additional components. This gives you a single, unified view of costs across your entire application, which makes it easy to monitor, understand, and debug your spend.

Token usage and costs are broken down into three categories:
**Input** : Tokens in the prompt sent to the model. Subtypes include: cache reads, text tokens, image tokens, etc
**Output** : Tokens generated in the response from the model. Subtypes include: reasoning tokens, text tokens, image tokens, etc
**Other** : Costs from tool calls, retrieval steps or any custom runs.
You can view detailed breakdowns by hovering over cost sections in the UI. When available, each section is further categorized by subtype.

excerpts:
With our OpenTelemetry (OTel) integration, you can standardize tracing across your stack and send traces to LangSmith — our testing & observability platform for the agent lifecycle — or other observability platforms.
With this update, LangSmith now offers a complete OpenTelemetry pipeline for LLM applications:
**LangChain instrumentation** : Automatically generate detailed traces from your LangChain or LangGraph applications
**LangSmith SDK** : Convert and transport these traces through our SDK using OpenTelemetry's standardized format
**LangSmith platform** : Ingest and visualize traces in a powerful, LLM-specific observability dashboard
This end-to-end integration unlocks several key benefits:
**Unified observability** : View your entire application stack—from LangChain components to underlying infrastructure—in a single, cohesive view
**Distributed tracing** : Follow requests as they move through your microservices architecture, with context propagation ensuring that related spans are linked to the same trace
**Interoperability** : Connect LangSmith with your existing observability tools and infrastructure through the OpenTelemetry standard, including platforms like Datadog, Grafana, and Jaeger
With this integration, you can trace the complete execution path of your LLM applications, from the initial prompt to the final response, with detailed visibility into each step along the way.
OpenTelemetry (OTel) is an open-source observability framework  that standardizes how telemetry data is collected, exported, and analyzed
For LLM applications, observability presents unique challenges. Traditional application monitoring focuses on errors and compliance with expected behaviors — however, LLM observability requires understanding multi-step workflows and monitoring dynamic, stochastic outputs with complex evaluation metrics that go beyond simple error rates.
OpenTelemetry addresses these challenges by providing a unified, vendor-neutral standard for instrumentation that works across different languages, frameworks, and backends.
LangSmith now provides end-to-end OpenTelemetry (OTel) support for applications built on LangChain and/or LangGraph.
citations.title: Introducing End-to-End OpenTelemetry Support in LangSmith

excerpts: The repo contains standard OpenTelemetry instrumentations for LLM providers and Vector DBs, as well as a Traceloop SDK that makes it easy to get started with OpenLLMetry, while still outputting standard OpenTelemetry data that can be connected to your observability stack.

OpenLLMetry is a set of extensions built on top of [OpenTelemetry](https://opentelemetry.io/) that gives you complete observability over your LLM application.

Because it uses OpenTelemetry under the hood, [it can be connected to your existing observability solutions](https://www.traceloop.com/docs/openllmetry/integrations/introduction) - Datadog, Honeycomb, and others.

excerpts: Your **Workflow** is the orchestration layer, the blueprint that defines the structure of your application. It needs to be deterministic so Temporal can help your agent survive through process crashes, outages, and other failures.

Deterministic execution unlocks durability. If the Workflow has to recover from a crash, it “replays” your agent’s progress to date. But the Workflow does not ask the agent for a new plan for decisions it has already made... The agent instead uses Temporal’s Event History as a record of past decisions. This makes the execution **durable** and is what makes the agent able to survive a crash and resume exactly where it left off.

Your **Workflow** is the orchestration layer, the blueprint that defines the structure of your application. It needs to be deterministic so Temporal can help your agent survive through process crashes, outages, and other failures.
**While Temporal requires that your Workflow code is deterministic, your AI Agent can absolutely make decisions based on non-deterministic LLM outcomes.**

This separation is *exactly* what makes Temporal perfect for AI agents. In fact, the longer running your agents are, the more valuable Temporal’s [Durable Execution](https://temporal.io/blog/what-is-durable-execution) superpowers become.
**Deterministic in execution** : An agent will always take the same path given the same context and LLM decisions
**BUT NOT predetermined** : You don't know what the LLM will decide until runtime

excerpts: Multi-agent architecture enables several powerful patterns. Here, I’ll start from the basics, and describe how Temporal can be used to make building multi-agent systems simple, durable, and fun.
One question I get asked often is, *“Should I build an agent as a Workflow or as an Activity?”*
For me, the answer is: it depends on *lifecycle and interaction:*
**Conversational Agents:** interactive, long running, orchestration: Workflows
**LLM Calls:** call to external system: Activities
**Simple Automation Agents:** short-lived, no interaction: Activities
**Long-Running Agents:** long duration, often have interaction: Workflows

excerpts:
.
**Layered termination strategy:
**Layer 4: Hard limits.** Absolute caps on iterations, time and cost. These fire regardless of what the agent thinks.
**Layer 5: Stuck detection.** Recognize patterns that indicate the agent can’t proceed: repeated errors, circular reasoning, repeated tool calls with same arguments. Route these to human review.

Layer 1: LLM self-assessment.** The agent explicitly evaluates whether the goal is achieved. This is a separate reasoning step, not embedded in the main loop: “Given the original goal and current state, is the task complete? Why or why not?”

**Layer 2: Programmatic verification.** Where possible, verify completion programmatically. If the goal was “create a file containing X,” check that the file exists and contains X. Don’t trust the agent’s claim.
**Layer 3: Progress detection.** Track whether the agent is making meaningful progress. If the last N steps haven’t changed the environment state meaningfully, prompt for clarification or terminate.
**You can’t define success.** Agents need termination conditions. If “done” is fuzzy or subjective, the agent will either stop too early or run forever.

**The Short Answer
** Combine goal-completion detection, iteration limits, time limits and explicit “I’m stuck” recognition — with the orchestrator as the final authority.
*

excerpts: **Layer 4: Hard limits.** Absolute caps on iterations, time and cost. These fire regardless of what the agent thinks.

.
**Layered termination strategy:
**Layer 5: Stuck detection.** Recognize patterns that indicate the agent can’t proceed: repeated errors, circular reasoning, repeated tool calls with same arguments. Route these to human review.
**Layer 3: Progress detection.** Track whether the agent is making meaningful progress. If the last N steps haven’t changed the environment state meaningfully, prompt for clarification or terminate.

**The Short Answer
** Combine goal-completion detection, iteration limits, time limits and explicit “I’m stuck” recognition — with the orchestrator as the final authority.
*

Layer 1: LLM self-assessment.** The agent explicitly evaluates whether the goal is achieved. This is a separate reasoning step, not embedded in the main loop: “Given the original goal and current state, is the task complete? Why or why not?”

**Layer 2: Programmatic verification.** Where possible, verify completion programmatically. If the goal was “create a file containing X,” check that the file exists and contains X. Don’t trust the agent’s claim.
**You can’t define success.** Agents need termination conditions. If “done” is fuzzy or subjective, the agent will either stop too early or run forever.

**The Complete Answer
** Over-reasoning is one of the most common failure modes I see. The agent thinks and thinks and thinks but never acts, or it creates elaborate plans for simple tasks

excerpts:
**The Speed Limit (Usage Throttling):** A **Limit of executions per minute** prevents "runaway" usage or an application bug from generating excessive, rapid calls.
**The Time Limit (Efficiency Control):** A **Time limit (hs)** (e.g., 24 hours) for a single conversation ensures that an agent isn't held in an open, expensive state indefinitely, which can consume unnecessary tokens.
**Monthly quota limit ($)** |Implements a **Hard Cap** on total monthly spend (The "Run" cost)
**Dynamic Model Selection:** Route simple, high-volume tasks (like FAQs) to cheaper models (e.g., GPT-4 mini) and reserve powerful models (like **Claude 4 Sonnet** ) only for complex reasoning tasks.
*Execution limits per minute** |Implements **API Rate Limiting** . Prevents excessive, rapid calls that could lead to budget spikes and API provider penalties.

excerpts:
**The Speed Limit (Usage Throttling):** A **Limit of executions per minute** prevents "runaway" usage or an application bug from generating excessive, rapid calls.
**The Time Limit (Efficiency Control):** A **Time limit (hs)** (e.g., 24 hours) for a single conversation ensures that an agent isn't held in an open, expensive state indefinitely, which can consume unnecessary tokens.
**Monthly quota limit ($)** |Implements a **Hard Cap** on total monthly spend (The "Run" cost)
**Dynamic Model Selection:** Route simple, high-volume tasks (like FAQs) to cheaper models (e.g., GPT-4 mini) and reserve powerful models (like **Claude 4 Sonnet** ) only for complex reasoning tasks.
*Execution limits per minute** |Implements **API Rate Limiting** . Prevents excessive, rapid calls that could lead to budget spikes and API provider penalties.

excerpts: Your **Workflow** is the orchestration layer, the blueprint that defines the structure of your application. It needs to be deterministic so Temporal can help your agent survive through process crashes, outages, and other failures.

Deterministic execution unlocks durability. If the Workflow has to recover from a crash, it “replays” your agent’s progress to date. But the Workflow does not ask the agent for a new plan for decisions it has already made... The agent instead uses Temporal’s Event History as a record of past decisions. This makes the execution **durable** and is what makes the agent able to survive a crash and resume exactly where it left off.

With this update, LangSmith now offers a complete OpenTelemetry pipeline for LLM applications:
**LangChain instrumentation** : Automatically generate detailed traces from your LangChain or LangGraph applications

excerpts: **Checkpointing** : AI applications beyond the simplest of demos make many calls to LLMs and tools, and they are increasingly leveraging other agents. In the event that the application stops before completion (due to both foreseen and unforeseen causes), checkpointing keeps the application from having to start from the beginning and rerun previous steps in the workflow.

ree.
If the application instance were to shut down (crash, be proactively cycled, or because you wanted to fix a bug even while the app was running), as soon as the application starts again, the state will be recreated and processing will pick up where it left off. When building Temporal applications,

For example, when a network glitch renders the LLM unavailable or when the LLM is rate-limiting requests, the Activity automatically retries those requests until conditions allow for completion. As the developer, you don’t write the retry logic; that behavior is handled by Temporal.

excerpts: LangSmith automatically records LLM token usage and costs for major providers, and also allows you to submit custom cost data for any additional components. This gives you a single, unified view of costs across your entire application, which makes it easy to monitor, understand, and debug your spend.

In the [LangSmith UI](https://smith.langchain.com) , you can explore usage and spend in three main ways: first by understanding how tokens and costs are broken down, then by viewing those details within individual traces, and finally by inspecting aggregated metrics in project stats and dashboards.
S

Token usage and costs are broken down into three categories:
**Input** : Tokens in the prompt sent to the model. Subtypes include: cache reads, text tokens, image tokens, etc
**Output** : Tokens generated in the response from the model. Subtypes include: reasoning tokens, text tokens, image tokens, etc
**Other** : Costs from tool calls, retrieval steps or any custom runs.
You can view detailed breakdowns by hovering over cost sections in the UI. When available, each section is further categorized by subtype.
A model pricing map is used to map model names to their per-token prices to compute costs from token counts. LangSmith’s [model pricing table](https://smith.l
compute cost automatically from token usage, you need to provide **token counts** , the **model and provider** and the **mode
A model pricing map is used to map model names to their per-token prices to compute costs from token counts.

basis.reasoning: The described risk centers on premature termination when an agent incorrectly believes it has achieved the goal. Directly relevant content notes that agents require explicit termination conditions; otherwise, the agent may stop too early or run indefinitely. This aligns with the risk by highlighting that fuzzy or subjective success criteria lead to premature stopping. Additional sources propose a multi-layered approach to termination: combining goal-completion checks, explicit time and iteration limits, and an explicit signal for being stuck, with the orchestrator acting as the final authority. This strengthens the termination framework and directly counters mis-termination by ensuring multiple, independent checks before termination. Further support comes from the idea that the agent should explicitly evaluate whether the goal is achieved and, if necessary, verify completion programmatically (e.g., verifying that a created file exists and contains the expected content). A layered termination strategy is proposed, along with progress detection, hard limits, and stuck-detection mechanisms, all of which provide concrete mechanisms to prevent premature termination and ensure corrective action or escalation. Additional excerpts discuss durable execution and exit semantics, which contextualize how termination and exit points are handled in practice, though they are less about the mis-termination risk itself.

excerpts: **You can’t define success.** Agents need termination conditions. If “done” is fuzzy or subjective, the agent will either stop too early or run forever.

**The Short Answer
** Combine goal-completion detection, iteration limits, time limits and explicit “I’m stuck” recognition — with the orchestrator as the final authority.
*

Layer 1: LLM self-assessment.** The agent explicitly evaluates whether the goal is achieved. This is a separate reasoning step, not embedded in the main loop: “Given the original goal and current state, is the task complete? Why or why not?”

**Layer 2: Programmatic verification.** Where possible, verify completion programmatically. If the goal was “create a file containing X,” check that the file exists and contains X. Don’t trust the agent’s claim.

.
**Layered termination strategy:
**Layer 3: Progress detection.** Track whether the agent is making meaningful progress. If the last N steps haven’t changed the environment state meaningfully, prompt for clarification or terminate.
**Layer 4: Hard limits.** Absolute caps on iterations, time and cost. These fire regardless of what the agent thinks.
**Layer 5: Stuck detection.** Recognize patterns that indicate the agent can’t proceed: repeated errors, circular reasoning, repeated tool calls with same arguments. Route these to human review.
citations.title: The Complete Agentic AI System Design Interview Guide 2026 | by TechEon | Jan, 2026 | Medium

excerpts: `"exit"` : LangGraph persists changes only when graph execution exits either successfully, with an error, or due to a human in the loop interrupt.
**Durable execution** is a technique in which a process or workflow saves its progress at key points, allowing it to pause and later resume exactly where it left off.
`"async"` : LangGraph persists changes asynchronously while the next step executes.
`"sync"` : LangGraph persists changes synchronously before the next step starts.

basis.reasoning: The strongest support comes from discussions of durable execution and crash recovery. One excerpt explains that when an application stops due to crash or intentional cycling, the state is recreated on restart and processing resumes from where it left off, which directly addresses termination and control in a resilient deployment. Another excerpt highlights checkpointing to avoid restarting from the very beginning, which is a core mechanism enabling controlled termination and safe resumption. A third excerpt emphasizes that the workflow must be deterministic so it can recover after a crash, while the agent itself can use non-deterministic outcomes; this separation clarifies where termination control lies (in the deterministic workflow) and how recovery proceeds. A fourth excerpt notes that if a crash occurs, the system replays progress but does not request a new plan for decisions already made by the agent, underscoring controlled continuity and exit/recovery semantics. excerpts: **Checkpointing** : AI applications beyond the simplest of demos make many calls to LLMs and tools, and they are increasingly leveraging other agents. In the event that the application stops before completion (due to both foreseen and unforeseen causes), checkpointing keeps the application from having to start from the beginning and rerun previous steps in the workflow.

For example, when a network glitch renders the LLM unavailable or when the LLM is rate-limiting requests, the Activity automatically retries those requests until conditions allow for completion. As the developer, you don’t write the retry logic; that behavior is handled by Temporal.

ree.

citations.title: Durable Execution meets AI: Why Temporal is ideal for AI agents & Generative AI Apps | Temporal

excerpts: Deterministic execution unlocks durability. If the Workflow has to recover from a crash, it “replays” your agent’s progress to date. But the Workflow does not ask the agent for a new plan for decisions it has already made... The agent instead uses Temporal’s Event History as a record of past decisions. This makes the execution **durable** and is what makes the agent able to survive a crash and resume exactly where it left off.

Your **Workflow** is the orchestration layer, the blueprint that defines the structure of your application. It needs to be deterministic so Temporal can help your agent survive through process crashes, outages, and other failures.
**While Temporal requires that your Workflow code is deterministic, your AI Agent can absolutely make decisions based on non-deterministic LLM outcomes.**

excerpts: **You can’t define success.** Agents need termination conditions. If “done” is fuzzy or subjective, the agent will either stop too early or run forever.

**The Short Answer
** Combine goal-completion detection, iteration limits, time limits and explicit “I’m stuck” recognition — with the orchestrator as the final authority.
*

Layer 1: LLM self-assessment.** The agent explicitly evaluates whether the goal is achieved. This is a separate reasoning step, not embedded in the main loop: “Given the original goal and current state, is the task complete? Why or why not?”

**Layer 2: Programmatic verification.** Where possible, verify completion programmatically. If the goal was “create a file containing X,” check that the file exists and contains X. Don’t trust the agent’s claim.

.
**Layered termination strategy:
**Layer 3: Progress detection.** Track whether the agent is making meaningful progress. If the last N steps haven’t changed the environment state meaningfully, prompt for clarification or terminate.
**Layer 4: Hard limits.** Absolute caps on iterations, time and cost. These fire regardless of what the agent thinks.
**Layer 5: Stuck detection.** Recognize patterns that indicate the agent can’t proceed: repeated errors, circular reasoning, repeated tool calls with same arguments. Route these to human review.

excerpts: `"exit"` : LangGraph persists changes only when graph execution exits either successfully, with an error, or due to a human in the loop interrupt.
**Durable execution** is a technique in which a process or workflow saves its progress at key points, allowing it to pause and later resume exactly where it left off.
`"async"` : LangGraph persists changes asynchronously while the next step executes.
`"sync"` : LangGraph persists changes synchronously before the next step starts.

Additionally, the emphasis on determinism in the workflow so that recovery can replay past decisions helps ensure termination decisions do not lead to inconsistent states after a crash. The discussion of replaying progress and the agent not needing a new plan for previously made decisions reinforces the idea of structured termination logic that safely exits and resumes without misalignment. Further, the ability for the system to automatically retry LLM calls when the service is unavailable contributes to a robust termination/exit strategy by handling partial failures gracefully rather than terminating outright. Finally, the note that the workflow can survive crashes and continue processing underlines the separation of concerns between termination control, decision history, and real-time agent behavior, which supports the concept of a layered termination mechanism with circuit-breaker-like properties embedded in resilience patterns.

excerpts: **Checkpointing** : AI applications beyond the simplest of demos make many calls to LLMs and tools, and they are increasingly leveraging other agents. In the event that the application stops before completion (due to both foreseen and unforeseen causes), checkpointing keeps the application from having to start from the beginning and rerun previous steps in the workflow.

ree.
If the application instance were to shut down (crash, be proactively cycled, or because you wanted to fix a bug even while the app was running), as soon as the application starts again, the state will be recreated and processing will pick up where it left off. When building Temporal applications,

For example, when a network glitch renders the LLM unavailable or when the LLM is rate-limiting requests, the Activity automatically retries those requests until conditions allow for completion. As the developer, you don’t write the retry logic; that behavior is handled by Temporal.
citations.title: Durable Execution meets AI: Why Temporal is ideal for AI agents & Generative AI Apps | Temporal

excerpts: Deterministic execution unlocks durability. If the Workflow has to recover from a crash, it “replays” your agent’s progress to date. But the Workflow does not ask the agent for a new plan for decisions it has already made... The agent instead uses Temporal’s Event History as a record of past decisions. This makes the execution **durable** and is what makes the agent able to survive a crash and resume exactly where it left off.

Your **Workflow** is the orchestration layer, the blueprint that defines the structure of your application. It needs to be deterministic so Temporal can help your agent survive through process crashes, outages, and other failures.
**While Temporal requires that your Workflow code is deterministic, your AI Agent can absolutely make decisions based on non-deterministic LLM outcomes.**
citations.title: Of course you can build dynamic AI agents with Temporal | Temporal

excerpts:
**The Speed Limit (Usage Throttling):** A **Limit of executions per minute** prevents "runaway" usage or an application bug from generating excessive, rapid calls.
**The Time Limit (Efficiency Control):** A **Time limit (hs)** (e.g., 24 hours) for a single conversation ensures that an agent isn't held in an open, expensive state indefinitely, which can consume unnecessary tokens.
**Monthly quota limit ($)** |Implements a **Hard Cap** on total monthly spend (The "Run" cost)
**Dynamic Model Selection:** Route simple, high-volume tasks (like FAQs) to cheaper models (e.g., GPT-4 mini) and reserve powerful models (like **Claude 4 Sonnet** ) only for complex reasoning tasks.
*Execution limits per minute** |Implements **API Rate Limiting** . Prevents excessive, rapid calls that could lead to budget spikes and API provider penalties.

excerpts: LangSmith automatically records LLM token usage and costs for major providers, and also allows you to submit custom cost data for any additional components. excerpts: LangSmith automatically records LLM token usage and costs for major providers, and also allows you to submit custom cost data for any additional components. This gives you a single, unified view of costs across your entire application, which makes it easy to monitor, understand, and debug your spend.

In the [LangSmith UI](https://smith.langchain.com) , you can explore usage and spend in three main ways: first by understanding how tokens and costs are broken down, then by viewing those details within individual traces, and finally by inspecting aggregated metrics in project stats and dashboards.
S

Token usage and costs are broken down into three categories:
**Input** : Tokens in the prompt sent to the model. Subtypes include: cache reads, text tokens, image tokens, etc
**Output** : Tokens generated in the response from the model. Subtypes include: reasoning tokens, text tokens, image tokens, etc
**Other** : Costs from tool calls, retrieval steps or any custom runs.
You can view detailed breakdowns by hovering over cost sections in the UI. When available, each section is further categorized by subtype.

compute cost automatically from token usage, you need to provide **token counts** , the **model and provider** and the **mode
