excerpts:
With this update, LangSmith now offers a complete OpenTelemetry pipeline for LLM applications:
**LangChain instrumentation** : Automatically generate detailed traces from your LangChain or LangGraph applications
**LangSmith SDK** : Convert and transport these traces through our SDK using OpenTelemetry's standardized format
**LangSmith platform** : Ingest and visualize traces in a powerful, LLM-specific observability dashboard
This end-to-end integration unlocks several key benefits:
**Unified observability** : View your entire application stack—from LangChain components to underlying infrastructure—in a single, cohesive view
**Distributed tracing** : Follow requests as they move through your microservices architecture, with context propagation ensuring that related spans are linked to the same trace
**Interoperability** : Connect LangSmith with your existing observability tools and infrastructure through the OpenTelemetry standard, including platforms like Datadog, Grafana, and Jaeger
With our OpenTelemetry (OTel) integration, you can standardize tracing across your stack and send traces to LangSmith — our testing & observability platform for the agent lifecycle — or other observability platforms.

excerpts:
**🎉 New** :
Our semantic conventions are now part of OpenTelemetry! Join the [discussion](https://github.com/open-telemetry/community/blob/1c71595874e5d125ca92ec3b0e948c4325161c8a/projects/llm-semconv.md) and help us shape the future of LLM observability.
OpenLLMetry is a set of extensions built on top of [OpenTelemetry](https://opentelemetry.io/) that gives you complete observability over your LLM application.

The repo contains standard OpenTelemetry instrumentations for LLM providers and Vector DBs, as well as a Traceloop SDK that makes it easy to get started with OpenLLMetry, while still outputting standard OpenTelemetry data that can be connected to your observability stack.

excerpts:
**The Speed Limit (Usage Throttling):** A **Limit of executions per minute** prevents "runaway" usage or an application bug from generating excessive, rapid calls.
**The Time Limit (Efficiency Control):** A **Time limit (hs)** (e.g., 24 hours) for a single conversation ensures that an agent isn't held in an open, expensive state indefinitely, which can consume unnecessary tokens.
**Monthly quota limit ($)** |Implements a **Hard Cap** on total monthly spend (The "Run" cost)
**Dynamic Model Selection:** Route simple, high-volume tasks (like FAQs) to cheaper models (e.g., GPT-4 mini) and reserve powerful models (like **Claude 4 Sonnet** ) only for complex reasoning tasks.
*Execution limits per minute** |Implements **API Rate Limiting** . Prevents excessive, rapid calls that could lead to budget spikes and API provider penalties.

excerpts: **Checkpointing** : AI applications beyond the simplest of demos make many calls to LLMs and tools, and they are increasingly leveraging other agents. In the event that the application stops before completion (due to both foreseen and unforeseen causes), checkpointing keeps the application from having to start from the beginning and rerun previous steps in the workflow.

ree.
If the application instance were to shut down (crash, be proactively cycled, or because you wanted to fix a bug even while the app was running), as soon as the application starts again, the state will be recreated and processing will pick up where it left off. When building Temporal applications,

For example, when a network glitch renders the LLM unavailable or when the LLM is rate-limiting requests, the Activity automatically retries those requests until conditions allow for completion. As the developer, you don’t write the retry logic; that behavior is handled by Temporal.
citations.title: Durable Execution meets AI: Why Temporal is ideal for AI agents & Generative AI Apps | Temporal

excerpts: Your **Workflow** is the orchestration layer, the blueprint that defines the structure of your application. It needs to be deterministic so Temporal can help your agent survive through process crashes, outages, and other failures.

Deterministic execution unlocks durability. If the Workflow has to recover from a crash, it “replays” your agent’s progress to date. But the Workflow does not ask the agent for a new plan for decisions it has already made... The agent instead uses Temporal’s Event History as a record of past decisions. This makes the execution **durable** and is what makes the agent able to survive a crash and resume exactly where it left off.
**While Temporal requires that your Workflow code is deterministic, your AI Agent can absolutely make decisions based on non-deterministic LLM outcomes.**
citations.title: Of course you can build dynamic AI agents with Temporal | Temporal

excerpts:
With this update, LangSmith now offers a complete OpenTelemetry pipeline for LLM applications:
**LangChain instrumentation** : Automatically generate detailed traces from your LangChain or LangGraph applications
**LangSmith SDK** : Convert and transport these traces through our SDK using OpenTelemetry's standardized format
**LangSmith platform** : Ingest and visualize traces in a powerful, LLM-specific observability dashboard
This end-to-end integration unlocks several key benefits:
**Unified observability** : View your entire application stack—from LangChain components to underlying infrastructure—in a single, cohesive view
**Distributed tracing** : Follow requests as they move through your microservices architecture, with context propagation ensuring that related spans are linked to the same trace
**Interoperability** : Connect LangSmith with your existing observability tools and infrastructure through the OpenTelemetry standard, including platforms like Datadog, Grafana, and Jaeger
With our OpenTelemetry (OTel) integration, you can standardize tracing across your stack and send traces to LangSmith — our testing & observability platform for the agent lifecycle — or other observability platforms.

excerpts: LangSmith automatically records LLM token usage and costs for major providers, and also allows you to submit custom cost data for any additional components. This gives you a single, unified view of costs across your entire application, which makes it easy to monitor, understand, and debug your spend.

In the [LangSmith UI](https://smith.langchain.com) , you can explore usage and spend in three main ways: first by understanding how tokens and costs are broken down, then by viewing those details within individual traces, and finally by inspecting aggregated metrics in project stats and dashboards.
S

Token usage and costs are broken down into three categories:
**Input** : Tokens in the prompt sent to the model. Subtypes include: cache reads, text tokens, image tokens, etc
**Output** : Tokens generated in the response from the model. Subtypes include: reasoning tokens, text tokens, image tokens, etc
**Other** : Costs from tool calls, retrieval steps or any custom runs.
You can view detailed breakdowns by hovering over cost sections in the UI. When available, each section is further categorized by subtype.

executive_summary.description: A high-level overview of the latest approaches to agentic AI system management in 2025-2026, covering termination, observability, budget governance, and crash recovery.

key_trends_2025_2026.description: A summary of the major trends shaping the agentic AI infrastructure landscape, including protocol convergence, the shift to specialized agents, and the rise of 'AgentOps'.

strategy_name.description: The name of the termination strategy or layer.

description.description: A detailed explanation of how the strategy functions to determine if an agent should stop.

implementation_level.description: Where the strategy is typically implemented (e.g., LLM self-assessment, Orchestrator-enforced).

agent_termination_strategies.description: A detailed breakdown of the latest approaches for agent termination and exit conditions. This includes layered strategies combining LLM self-assessment, programmatic verification, progress detection, hard limits (time, cost, iteration), and stuck-pattern recognition.

common_termination_failures.description: An analysis of common failure modes related to agent termination, such as stopping too early, stopping too late, infinite planning loops, and misinterpreting task completion.

observability_patterns_overview.description: An overview of new observability patterns for LLM agents, emphasizing the shift from simple logging to structured tracing of agent decision-making processes, including thoughts, tool calls, and state changes.

standardization_status.description: The current status of the OpenTelemetry GenAI Special Interest Group (SIG) and its efforts to standardize agent observability.

key_semantic_conventions.description: Details on the specific semantic conventions being developed, such as for agent applications, agent frameworks, LLMs, and VectorDBs.

instrumentation_approaches.description: A description of the different ways to instrument agents, including baked-in framework instrumentation versus external OpenTelemetry libraries.

platform_name.description: The name of the observability platform (e.g., LangSmith, Braintrust, Langfuse).

key_features.description: A summary of the platform's core features, such as tracing, evaluation suites, and CI/CD integration.

opentelemetry_support.description: Details on the platform's integration with OpenTelemetry and its compliance with GenAI semantic conventions.

cost_attribution_capabilities.description: Description of how the platform tracks and attributes costs, including token usage and custom costs.

observability_platform_features.description: A comparative analysis of how key platforms handle observability. For each platform (e.g., LangSmith, Braintrust, Langfuse, Arize AI/Phoenix, OpenLLMetry), detail their specific features for tracing, evaluation, cost attribution, CI/CD integration, and support for OpenTelemetry.

approach_name.description: The name of the budget governance method (e.g., Monthly Quota Limit, Per-Task Budget).

description.description: An explanation of what the approach does and the problem it solves, such as preventing budget overruns.

implementation_type.description: The technical implementation of the approach (e.g., Hard Cap, API Rate Limiting, Real-time Monitoring).

budget_governance_approaches.description: A list of production-ready approaches for implementing budget governance across multi-agent workflows. This includes per-task budgets, per-agent quotas, dynamic budget reallocation, real-time spend monitoring, and policy-driven cost controls.

lever_name.description: The name of the technical parameter or design choice used for cost control (e.g., Dynamic Model Selection).

technical_impact.description: The technical effect of using this lever, such as setting the base cost per token or implementing API rate limiting.

savings_tactic.description: The specific strategy for saving costs with this lever, like routing simple tasks to cheaper models.

crash_recovery_and_durability_patterns.description: An overview of new approaches to crash recovery and ensuring agent durability. This covers circuit breaker patterns, stateful recovery mechanisms, and the concept of durable execution.

core_concept.description: The main principle behind LangGraph's approach, which is 'Durable Execution'.

mechanism.description: How durability is achieved, primarily through checkpointers that save workflow progress to a durable store.

key_features.description: Specific features that enable recovery, such as different persistence modes (sync, async, exit) and support for interrupts.

risks_and_mitigations.description: Potential issues like the 'replay risk' with non-idempotent operations and the need to wrap side effects in tasks.

core_concept.description: The main principle behind Temporal's approach, which is 'Durable Execution' via event sourcing.

architecture.description: The architectural separation of deterministic 'Workflows' (orchestration) from non-deterministic 'Activities' (LLM calls, tool use).

recovery_mechanism.description: How recovery works by replaying the recorded Event History to restore the exact state of the workflow before a crash.

benefits.description: Advantages of this approach, such as fault-oblivious code, built-in retries, and the ability to handle long-running processes.

missing_framework_decision_points.description: An analysis of the decision points and capabilities that are currently missing or underdeveloped in common agentic frameworks regarding termination, observability, and budget governance.

risk_name.description: The name of the specific risk or failure mode (e.g., Silent Wrong Answers, Goal Drift, Agent Sprawl).

description.description: A detailed explanation of the risk and how it manifests in production systems.

detection_and_mitigation.description: Common strategies for detecting and mitigating the risk, such as sampling-based audits or periodic re-grounding against original objectives.

agentic_architecture_risks.description: A summary of key risks and challenges in production agentic systems, such as silent wrong answers, goal drift, assumption propagation, agent sprawl, and expanded security attack surfaces.

guidance_area.description: The area of focus for the guidance (e.g., Identity Management, Operations, Governance).

recommendation.description: The specific best practice or action to take, such as 'Treat agents as first-class identities'.

implementation_details.description: Notes on how to implement the recommendation, like registering agents in a directory service and assigning scoped roles.

production_deployment_guidance.description: A list of concrete best practices for deploying and managing agents in production, including treating agents as first-class identities, implementing human-in-the-loop approval gates, and building a central 'AgentOps' function for lifecycle management.

scenario.description: The situation or type of task where an agentic architecture is not a good fit.

reasoning.description: Why agents are inefficient or risky in this scenario, such as adding latency and unpredictability to deterministic problems.

alternative_approach.description: The recommended alternative technology or pattern to use instead (e.g., traditional workflow engine, state machine).
